breakdown: Past participle suffix (var > vur + ta > vuṭa).

āvuṭā.gram: past participle of āvarati, feminine singular or masculine plural
breakdown: Past participle suffix with plural/feminine ending.

āvāhavivāhakānaṁ.meaning: of marriages and weddings; concerning the giving and taking in marriage (finding a suitable wife)
breakdown: Wedding; giving away (a daughter) in marriage (vi + vāhaka).

āya.meaning: gain, profit; (in the phrase bahujanahitāya) for the welfare and happiness of many people
āya.gram: masculine noun (in compounds); here read as the dative ending of the phrase
breakdown: Root: to go. āya: income, what comes in — gain.
āya.note: uncertain: the lexicon line glosses the phrase context “many + people + happiness + for” (bahujanahitāya), of which -āya is the dative ending.

āyamukhāni.meaning: inlets; the entrances of increase (channels by which income flows in)
breakdown: Mouth, entrance. Together: inlet, channel of inflow.

āyasmā.meaning: the Venerable (respectful title for a monk)
breakdown: Life, long life (the -s- belongs to the stem āyas-).
Possessive suffix: possessed of (long) life — venerable.

āyasmā the.summary: A glossary fragment; the entry is simply āyasmā, “the Venerable”.

āyatana.meaning: sense sphere: the sense organs (internal) and their respective objects (external)
āyatana.summary: The six internal āyatanas are the sense organs (eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, mind) and the six external ones their objects.
breakdown: Noun suffix: the sphere of extension — sense base.
āyatana.goenka: The āyatanas are the six internal sense organs — eye, ear, nose, tongue, body and mind — together with their six external objects. In this tradition they matter practically: every contact of a sense organ with its object gives rise to sensation (vedanā), and it is at this point that the meditator, observing with equanimity, can break the chain of blind reaction.

āyatanānaṁ.meaning: of the sense spheres; of the sense organs and their objects
āyatanānaṁ.gram: neuter noun, genitive/dative plural of āyatana

āyatiṁ.meaning: in the future; for the future
āyatiṁ.gram: feminine noun āyati, accusative singular used adverbially
breakdown: Root: to stretch. āyati: what stretches ahead — the future.

āyo.gram: masculine noun, nominative singular (ā + √i, to go)
breakdown: Root: to go. āya: what comes in — income.

āyuno.meaning: of life; of the life span, of age

āyuppamāṇaṁ.meaning: the life span; the measure or period of life
breakdown: Measure, extent (the pp is junction doubling).

āyusā.meaning: with one's life; in respect of life span

āyusā with.summary: A glossary fragment; the entry is simply āyusā, “with one's life”.

āyuvaṇṇādipariyānikathā.meaning: talk that turns around age, complexion and so forth; a sermon revolving on life span, beauty and the like

āyuvaṇṇādipari­.meaning: talk that turns around age, complexion and so forth (first part of āyuvaṇṇādipariyānikathā, broken across a line)
āyuvaṇṇādipari­.summary: A typographic line-break fragment; the full word is āyuvaṇṇādipariyānikathā.
āyuvaṇṇādipari­.gram: fragment of a feminine compound noun
breakdown: Turning round (the remainder -yānikathā completes the word).
āyuvaṇṇādipari­.note: uncertain: this headword is a hyphenation fragment from the printed source; the full word is āyuvaṇṇādipariyānikathā.

āyāsitattaṁ.meaning: the state of trouble; the condition of sorrow and distress

breakdown: Root: to exert, weary. āyāsa: weariness, trouble, distress.

āṭānāṭiyasuttaṁ,.meaning: the Āṭānāṭiya Sutta (a protective discourse), in the Pāthikavagga of the Dīghanikāya
breakdown: Relating to Āṭānāṭā (the city of the Four Great Kings, from which the protective verses are named).

ūhaññī.gram: verb, aorist of ūhaññati (passive of ud + √han)
breakdown: Root: to strike. Passive hañña: to be struck up — disturbed, stirred.

ḍayhamānena.meaning: by what is burning, consuming, tormenting; by (one) being burned
ḍayhamānena.gram: present participle (passive) of ḍayhati (√ḍah), instrumental singular
breakdown: Root: to burn. Passive stem ḍayha: to be burned.

ṭhapana.gram: neuter action noun, from ṭhapeti (causative of √ṭhā)
breakdown: Root: to stand. Causative ṭhape: to place, set.
Action-noun suffix: the placing aside, omission.

ṭhapanā.gram: feminine action noun, from ṭhapeti (causative of √ṭhā)
breakdown: Root: to stand. Causative ṭhape: to place.
Feminine action-noun suffix: the act of placing.

ṭhapetvāposathagge.meaning: having placed (it) in the Uposatha hall
ṭhapetvāposathagge.gram: sandhi phrase: gerund + locative noun (ṭhapetvā + uposathagge)
breakdown: The Uposatha observance day (the initial u- merges by sandhi with the preceding -ā).
Hall, house (here locative agge): the Uposatha hall.

ṭhapitāni.meaning: left out, placed aside; set up
ṭhapitāni.gram: past participle of ṭhapeti, neuter nominative/accusative plural
breakdown: Root: to stand. Causative ṭhape: to place.

ṭhitiyā.meaning: for the stability, for the persistence (of); of the maintenance
ṭhitiyā.gram: feminine abstract noun ṭhiti, dative/genitive singular
breakdown: Feminine abstract-noun suffix: standing — stability, persistence.

ṭhānehi.meaning: by these motivators, motivating conditions; from these causes or grounds (that instigate unwholesome actions)
ṭhānehi.summary: In this context the four “grounds” are the motivators of wrong action: greed, hatred, delusion — and fear as the fourth.
breakdown: Root: to stand. ṭhāna: place, ground, occasion; here motive, instigating condition.
ṭhānehi.goenka: Klaus explains that ṭhānā here are translated as motivators or motivating conditions — the motives, causes or instigators that stimulate unwholesome actions. The fourth motivator, fear, is added to the three roots of evil usually mentioned: lobhamūla (greed), dosamūla (hatred) and mohamūla (delusion). The commentary explains fear as the potential harm that may occur to oneself if one does not act accordingly.

ṭhānesu.meaning: in the places; at the occasions
breakdown: Root: to stand. ṭhāna: place, occasion.

ṭhānārahenā.meaning: with what is worthy of the position; by (conduct) fit for one's standing
breakdown: Instrumental singular ending (with metrically long final vowel).

‘asucikhādako’..meaning: “an eater of filth”; one who feeds on impurity (a term of abuse, quoted)
‘asucikhādako’..gram: masculine compound noun, nominative singular, quoted with punctuation
breakdown: Eater, one who chews (√khād, to eat).
‘asucikhādako’..note: uncertain: the lexicon record carries no gloss for this quoted headword; the meaning is derived from the transparent compound asuci + khādaka.
